html:
Sunlands Technology Group (STG) Thesis

STG is a tiny, ignored Chinese ADR that the market is valuing as if its adult education business is in terminal decline and its cash is unusable. That is too harsh. At a current price of about $3.15 and a market cap near $42 million, Sunlands trades at roughly 0.8x 2025 earnings, 2.0x 2025 free cash flow, and about 0.31x book value, despite reporting 2025 revenue growth, higher margins, and a debt-free balance sheet excluding leases.

The variant view is not that Sunlands is a clean net-net liquidation. It is not. Deferred revenue and other liabilities matter, and the old thesis overstated “net cash after all liabilities.” The stronger thesis is that STG is a profitable, high-gross-margin adult education platform being priced like a busted K-12 tutoring stock. The company ended 2025 with $116 million of cash, restricted cash, and short-term investments, plus $46 million of long-term investments, against $20 million of lease liabilities and no financial debt. Total shareholder equity was about $135 million, or roughly $10 per ADS, versus a $3 stock.

The business is also not dead. In 2025, revenue rose 1.5% to RMB2.02 billion, gross margin expanded to 86.9%, operating income rose to RMB444 million, and net income rose 6.9% to RMB366 million, or about $3.88 per ADS after the ADS ratio. Operating cash flow was positive at RMB147 million, or about $21 million, with minimal capex. These are not numbers that belong at a 0.8x P/E unless investors believe the cash is fake, trapped, or about to be destroyed.

The market’s concern is visible and legitimate: forward billings are weakening. Q4 2025 gross billings fell to RMB306 million from RMB412 million, new enrollments fell sharply, deferred revenue dropped to RMB585 million, and management guided Q1 2026 revenue down 9.8% to 13.9% year over year. That is why the stock has been crushed. But the key distinction is that Sunlands is intentionally moving away from scale-at-any-cost acquisition toward higher-quality revenue, lower sales spending, and interest/professional learning categories. Degree and diploma programs were only 13.5% of 2025 revenue, while interest, professional skills, and certification prep were 73.9% of revenue. This is a different business than the old Chinese exam-prep label implies.

The most interesting forward angle is senior interest learning. China’s elderly education market is still early, public supply is constrained, and online delivery fills a real access gap. Management says senior learners are becoming a core growth driver, with high engagement and repeat participation. That is supported by broader Chinese market data showing the 60-plus population reached roughly 310 million by year-end 2024 and that elderly education supply still lags demand. Sunlands is not just selling one-off test prep anymore. It is trying to build recurring adult learning communities around arts, culture, skills, certifications, and AI-enabled personalization.

Capital allocation is the swing factor. Sunlands has shown willingness to return capital, including a 2022 special dividend and buybacks, but recent buybacks have been too small relative to the opportunity. As of year-end 2025, it had repurchased only 815,399 ADSs for $4.8 million under a $15 million authorization that had been extended through December 2025. A renewed buyback at $3 would be wildly accretive. A meaningful tender or dividend would force the valuation gap to close. If management keeps hoarding cash and merely talks about “prudent” investment, the discount can persist.

Retail and institutional sponsorship are almost nonexistent. The float is tiny, average volume is only a few thousand shares, short interest is negligible, there is no real analyst coverage, and Reddit/social chatter is mostly automated news reposts. That cuts both ways. It makes the stock hard to own and easy to ignore, but it also means there is no crowded bull story. A small amount of real capital return or one clean quarter stabilizing billings could move the stock disproportionately.

My base case assumes 2026 revenue declines, but the business remains profitable because cost of revenue is low and marketing spend can be flexed. If 2026 net income falls to $30 million to $38 million and free cash flow falls to $12 million to $18 million, STG still trades at only 1.1x to 1.4x forward earnings and 2.3x to 3.5x forward FCF. That is absurdly cheap for a company with a large liquid balance sheet, no financial debt, and positive operating cash flow.

The main risks are VIE/China ADR governance, capital controls, weak liquidity, and a faster billings collapse. The thesis breaks if gross billings continue falling 20% to 30%, cash flow disappears, and management refuses to renew capital returns. The book value is also not a guaranteed liquidation value, because deferred revenue, leases, and long-lived assets require haircuts. But the current price already assumes a severe discount to both earnings power and asset value.

Targets from $3.15 current price:

Bear case: $1.80, about 43% downside. Billings keep collapsing, 2026 FCF approaches zero, the buyback is not renewed, and the market values STG at a deeper China ADR discount.

Base case: $8.00, about 154% upside. Earnings normalize around $2.25 to $2.75 per ADS, FCF remains positive, and the stock re-rates to roughly 3x depressed earnings or 0.8x book.

Bull case: $12.50, about 297% upside. Senior/interest learning stabilizes billings, AI-enabled product development improves retention, and management renews aggressive repurchases or announces a larger tender/dividend. At that point the stock can trade near tangible asset value while still leaving the operating business at a very low multiple.
